title: Emergency Guide: Cat Straining to Urinate (Urethral Obstruction)
meta_desc: If your cat is posturing to pee but nothing is coming out, it is a life-threatening emergency. Learn the signs and why you must act now.
content_html:
What is happening to your cat?

When a cat is repeatedly posturing in the litter box for several minutes without producing urine, it usually indicates a urethral obstruction, often referred to as a "blocked cat." This occurs when the tube that carries urine out of the bladder becomes physically blocked by crystals, mucus, or inflammation. This condition is part of Feline Lower Urinary Tract Disease (FLUTD) and prevents the body from being able to clear toxic waste products.

Is this a veterinary emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is High. A urinary blockage is a life-threatening medical emergency. If a cat cannot urinate, toxins build up in the bloodstream and potassium levels spike, which can lead to kidney failure and fatal heart failure within 24 to 48 hours. If you notice your cat straining, crying out in the litter box, or acting lethargic, you should go to an emergency veterinary clinic immediately.

How a photo or video helps triage

If you are consulting with a remote triage professional, providing a photo or short video of your cat's posture or the state of the litter box can be incredibly helpful. Seeing the specific "hunching" posture helps experts differentiate between constipation and a urinary emergency. Furthermore, a photo of any small droplets of urine—especially if they are tinged with blood—helps the veterinarian assess the severity of the inflammation and the potential type of obstruction.

faqs.question: Can I wait until the morning to see a vet?
faqs.answer: No. Urinary obstructions are time-sensitive and life-threatening. Every hour counts to prevent permanent kidney damage or cardiac arrest.

faqs.question: Why is this happening mostly to male cats?
faqs.answer: Male cats have a much longer and narrower urethra than female cats, making them significantly more prone to physical blockages from stones or inflammatory debris.

faqs.question: What are other signs of a blockage?
faqs.answer: In addition to straining, you may notice your cat licking their genital area excessively, vocalizing in pain, hiding, or vomiting as the toxin levels rise.
